topic_name: Undefined Terms: Points, Lines, and Planes
hook: You already know how to measure lengths, draw shapes, and work with coordinates on a grid. But here's a question that might trip you up: what exactly IS a line? Not a line segment with two endpoints, but a true geometric line. If you try to define it using other geometric words — like 'a straight path between two points' — you'll quickly realize you've used words like 'straight' and 'points' that also need definitions. So how do mathematicians build geometry from the ground up without going in circles?
concept: In geometry, some ideas are so fundamental that we accept them without formal definition — these are called undefined terms. The three undefined terms are point, line, and plane. A point marks an exact location in space and has no size. A line is a straight, one-dimensional figure extending infinitely in both directions, typically written as $\overleftrightarrow{AB}$. A plane is a flat, two-dimensional surface extending infinitely, named by three non-collinear points or a capital letter.
anatomy: Each undefined term has specific notation and characteristics worth naming precisely. A point is labeled with a single capital letter, such as point $A$ or point $P$, and is represented by a dot — it has no length, width, or thickness. A line is named by any two points on it, written as $\overleftrightarrow{AB}$, and contains infinitely many points; the arrows on both ends signal infinite extension in two directions. A plane is named either by a single italicized capital letter (plane $m$) or by three non-collinear points it contains (plane $ABC$); it has infinite length and width but zero thickness, like an idealized flat sheet.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Identify: point } Q, \text{ line } \overleftrightarrow{RS}, \text{ and plane } ABC
worked_example.description_latex: We are reading a geometric figure and naming each undefined term correctly. Notice that each object uses a specific notation — a single letter for a point, two letters with a double-headed arrow for a line, and three letters (or one letter) for a plane. Recognizing these notations is the first skill to build.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Point } Q \text{ — a single location, no size, labeled with one capital letter}
worked_example.description_latex: A point describes only a position in space, nothing more. It has no dimension at all — no length, no width, no depth — which is why we represent it as a dot and label it with just one capital letter like $Q$.

worked_example.expression_latex: \overleftrightarrow{RS} \text{ — passes through } R \text{ and } S, \text{ extends infinitely in both directions}
worked_example.description_latex: The notation $\overleftrightarrow{RS}$ tells you two things: the line passes through points $R$ and $S$, and the double-headed arrow means it never stops in either direction. Remember, a line is not a segment — it has no endpoints.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Plane } ABC \text{ — determined by three non-collinear points } A, B, \text{ and } C
worked_example.description_latex: Three points that do not all lie on the same line (non-collinear) determine exactly one plane, just as a three-legged stool never wobbles. If $A$, $B$, and $C$ were collinear (all on one line), they would not uniquely identify a single flat surface, which is why we require them to be non-collinear.

partial_example.expression_latex: \text{Given figure: points } D, E, F \text{ and a flat surface labeled } m
partial_example.description_latex: We are given a geometric figure with three labeled points and a flat surface. Our job is to name the plane two different ways — once using the letter label, and once using the three points. Let's start with the easier naming method.
Since the flat surface is already labeled with the letter $m$, we can call it plane $m$ directly. This is the single-letter naming method for a plane.

Now write the second name for this plane using the three points $D$, $E$, and $F$. Remember the rule: the three points must be non-collinear, and you write them as plane $DEF$ (any order of the three letters is acceptable). Why do the points need to be non-collinear?

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{Name the undefined term represented by a single dot labeled } P.
\text{Write the correct notation for the line passing through points } X \text{ and } Y.
\text{A flat surface contains points } A, B, \text{ and } C \text{ which are non-collinear. Name the plane two ways, given it is also labeled plane } n.
\text{Points } J, K, \text{ and } L \text{ all lie on } \overleftrightarrow{JK}. \text{ Can these three points determine a unique plane? Explain why or why not.}
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{No — collinear points lie on one line and do not determine a unique plane; infinitely many planes contain the same line.}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{A surveyor places three stakes in the ground at locations } P, Q, \text{ and } R \text{ that are not in a straight line. How many flat planes do these three stakes determine, and what is that plane called?}

common_mistakes: Students often confuse a line with a line segment, drawing endpoints on both sides of a line. A true line ($\overleftrightarrow{AB}$) has no endpoints and extends infinitely; always check that your notation includes arrows, not tick marks or dots at the ends.

Students sometimes try to name a plane using only two points, writing something like 'plane $AB$.' A plane requires three non-collinear points (or a single letter label) because two points only determine a line, not a unique flat surface — always use three non-collinear points or the given letter.

Students frequently assume that three any points determine a unique plane, forgetting the non-collinear requirement. If all three points lie on the same line, infinitely many planes pass through that line, so the three points do not pin down a single plane; always verify the points are non-collinear before naming a plane.
untested_variants: Identifying how many points, lines, or planes are determined by intersecting figures (e.g., two distinct planes intersecting in exactly one line).
Using coordinate geometry to verify whether three given coordinate points such as $(1,2,0)$, $(3,4,0)$, $(5,6,0)$ are collinear or non-collinear before naming a plane.
Applying postulates about points, lines, and planes — such as 'through any two points there is exactly one line' — to justify steps in a geometric argument or proof.
Distinguishing between collinear points (points on the same line) and coplanar points (points on the same plane), and identifying which sets of labeled points in a 3D figure satisfy each condition.
